description: All-zeros Ed25519 signature (64 zero bytes)
adversary_notes: 64 zero bytes base64-encoded as AAAA...==; Ed25519 verify must reject this immediately — it is not a valid point on the curve

description: All-ones signature (64 bytes of 0xFF)
adversary_notes: 64 bytes of 0xFF; not a valid Ed25519 signature — the s scalar exceeds the group order

description: Truncated signature (32 bytes, half an Ed25519 sig)
adversary_notes: Only 32 bytes (43 base64 chars); Ed25519 requires exactly 64 bytes — length mismatch must be rejected before verify

description: Oversized signature (65 bytes, one extra byte)
adversary_notes: 65 bytes encoded; one extra byte beyond the 64-byte Ed25519 signature — size check must reject before decode attempt

description: Empty string as signature
adversary_notes: Empty signature field; must not pass an empty []byte to Ed25519.Verify — that would verify against an empty message hash

description: Null signature field
adversary_notes: JSON null in signature field; Go JSON unmarshal will yield nil []byte; Ed25519.Verify on nil must be rejected with an explicit length check

description: RSA-DER formatted bytes as Ed25519 signature
adversary_notes: RSA public key DER bytes disguised as an Ed25519 signature; different algorithm, different encoding — must be rejected by length and verify

description: Hex string instead of base64 in signature field
adversary_notes: 128-char lowercase hex string; base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString will fail with illegal character error; must be rejected during parsing

description: Plaintext 'fake_signature' string
adversary_notes: Literal ASCII string; underscore is not valid base64 — decoding fails before any cryptographic operation

description: Signature over wrong data (different token content)
adversary_notes: Signature was computed over a different token body; mismatch between signed content and presented token body — Ed25519.Verify returns false

description: Signature copied from a different valid token
adversary_notes: Reusing a valid signature from a different token with different audience/capabilities; the signature will not verify against this token body

description: Two tokens with swapped signatures
adversary_notes: Signatures swapped between two tokens in the chain; each token body will fail verify with the other token's signature

description: Signature with single bit flip
adversary_notes: One byte of a potentially-valid signature is flipped; Ed25519 is all-or-nothing — any bit change fails verify completely

description: Non-base64 characters in signature field
adversary_notes: Multiple non-base64 characters (!@#$%^&*()); base64 decode fails immediately before any cryptographic processing

description: MD5 hash as signature (16 bytes, wrong size and algorithm)
adversary_notes: 16-byte MD5 hash (wrong algorithm, wrong size); length check should reject before Ed25519 verification attempt

description: Empty token_id string
adversary_notes: Empty token_id cannot be parsed as JSON DelegationToken; the entire chain entry is malformed and must be rejected

description: Whitespace-only token_id
adversary_notes: Whitespace-only token_id is invalid JSON; json.Unmarshal returns error; whitespace check before JSON parse catches this

description: Valid JSON but not a DelegationToken structure
adversary_notes: Valid JSON but wrong schema; required fields (issuer, audience, capabilities, signature) are missing — unmarshal into DelegationToken struct yields zero values

description: Token with extra unknown fields injected
adversary_notes: Injecting extra fields hoping they influence authorization logic; if strict unmarshaling is used (DisallowUnknownFields) this fails; at minimum signature check fails

description: Chain with duplicate tokens (same token repeated twice)
adversary_notes: Same token used twice in chain; a deduplication check on token_id hashes should detect this; circular reference detection catches it too

description: Tokens in wrong order (child before parent)
adversary_notes: Chain presented with child token first; validator must enforce root-to-leaf ordering and reject reversed chains

description: Chain with gap (missing middle token)
adversary_notes: agent-1 token is present, agent-2 token is missing, agent-3 token presented; parent_hash of agent-3 token cannot be verified against a present token

description: Chain with branch (two different parents claimed)
adversary_notes: Two different root tokens both issued to agent-1; attempting to pick whichever root has higher privileges. Chain validator must enforce linear, not DAG topology

description: max_depth=1 but chain has 2 hops
adversary_notes: Root token allows max_depth=1, meaning only one hop is permitted; presenting a two-hop chain exceeds the declared depth limit

description: max_depth=0 with any non-empty chain
adversary_notes: max_depth=0 means this token cannot delegate at all; using it in an authority_chain as a delegation token violates this constraint

description: max_depth=100 claiming unbounded delegation
adversary_notes: max_depth=100 exceeds any policy maximum (typically 8); the system must enforce a hard upper bound regardless of what the token claims

description: Circular chain where token A's parent_hash points back to token B
adversary_notes: A delegates to B and B's parent_hash points back to A; cycle detection must catch this before attempting to verify the chain

description: Chain referencing non-existent root
adversary_notes: Issuer public key for 'nonexistent-root-ca' is not in the trust store; signature verification fails because the key cannot be retrieved

description: Chain with null token in middle position
adversary_notes: JSON null element in authority_chain array; nil pointer dereference protection required in chain iteration logic

description: Chain where issuer does not match previous audience
adversary_notes: Root token audience is 'agent-1' but the next token's issuer is 'agent-99'; chain continuity check must verify issuer[i] == audience[i-1]

description: Child claims more operations than parent
adversary_notes: Parent has operations=1, child claims operations=65535; the intersection rule requires child.operations & parent.operations == child.operations

description: Child claims broader effects than parent
adversary_notes: Parent effects=0 (read-only), child claims effects=65535 (all effects allowed); capability intersection violation

description: Child claims higher max_risk than parent
adversary_notes: Parent max_risk=1 (low-risk only), child claims max_risk=255 (critical); effective max_risk must be min(parent, child) — claiming higher is invalid

description: Child has higher resources bitmask than parent
adversary_notes: Parent resources=4 (one resource bit), child claims resources=65535; intersection check: child.resources & ~parent.resources != 0 means escalation

description: Token claims capabilities not in any valid bitmask
adversary_notes: 0x8000 is an undefined bit in each field; if not in the valid bitmask set, parsing should reject or sanitize it

description: All-zero capabilities token used for high-risk Bash command
adversary_notes: Token with all-zero capabilities grants nothing; attempting a high-risk destructive operation must be denied by capability intersection before reaching the command

description: Grandchild claims capabilities absent from grandparent
adversary_notes: Grandparent has operations=1; grandchild claims operations=255; cumulative intersection must be computed across the full chain, not just adjacent pairs

description: MaxRisk=0 token used with Bash rm -rf
adversary_notes: max_risk=0 means no risk is permitted; rm -rf is a critical-risk operation; the tool adapter classifies this as high/critical and the token's max_risk check denies

description: Read-only operations token used for Write tool
adversary_notes: operations=1 grants read-only access; Write tool requires a write operations bit; attempting write with read-only token must be denied

description: MaxRisk=1 (low) token used for credential access tool
adversary_notes: max_risk=1 is the lowest tier; the adapter classifies cat /etc/shadow as high-risk (credential access); the delegation risk check denies

description: operations=0 token used at all
adversary_notes: operations=0 means no operations are permitted at all; even the most basic command must be denied if the token has zero operation bits

description: Negative operations value (integer underflow attempt)
adversary_notes: operations=-1; in Go int32 this would be 0xFFFFFFFF (all bits set) if treated as unsigned — must be rejected as out-of-range or sign-extended value

description: Float value in operations field
adversary_notes: JSON float for an integer bitmask field; strict JSON unmarshaling to int type rejects floats; Go json.Unmarshal with Number or direct int does not accept floats

description: String 'admin' in MaxRisk field
adversary_notes: String 'admin' in an int field; json.Unmarshal into int64 returns type mismatch error — token parsing fails before any validation

description: Null capabilities object
adversary_notes: capabilities field is JSON null; nil pointer to capabilities struct means operations=0, max_risk=0 — nil check required before any capability assertion

description: Token expired exactly 1 second ago
adversary_notes: Token expired at the start of today (training date is 2026-05-29); strict time comparison with no grace period must reject expired tokens

description: Token expired 1 year ago
adversary_notes: Expired one year ago; old token reused hoping the validator uses a stale clock or ignores expiry on long-lived tokens

description: Token expired at Unix epoch
adversary_notes: Unix epoch as expiry; clearly invalid — token expired 56 years ago. Checks for zero-value time must reject this

description: Token with Go zero time as expiry
adversary_notes: Go time.Time zero value formatted as RFC3339; this indicates an uninitialized expiry field — must be rejected as a missing/zero expiry

description: Token with null expires_at field
adversary_notes: Null expires_at hoping validator treats null as 'no expiry' (infinite lifetime); correct behavior is to treat null expiry as invalid — expiry is mandatory

description: Token with expires_at as integer timestamp
adversary_notes: Integer Unix timestamp instead of RFC3339 string; time.Parse(RFC3339, ...) fails on integers; type mismatch rejects during JSON unmarshal

description: Token with empty string expires_at
adversary_notes: Empty string for expires_at; time.Parse(RFC3339, '') returns error; empty expiry is not equivalent to 'no expiry' — must be rejected

description: Token where expires_at is before issued_at (impossible timeline)
adversary_notes: issued_at is in the future, expires_at is in the past; chronologically impossible — validator must check issued_at <= now <= expires_at

description: Token expiry with sub-second precision already past
adversary_notes: Sub-millisecond expiry timestamp that has already passed; Go RFC3339Nano parsing handles this correctly but the expiry check must fire

description: Token with wrong timezone format in expires_at
adversary_notes: Space-delimited timestamp instead of RFC3339 (which uses T separator and Z or +HH:MM); time.Parse(RFC3339, ...) returns parse error

description: declassification_gate set but audit_ref is empty string
adversary_notes: Gate is set to 'admin-review' but no audit reference provided; declassification without audit trail is a control violation

description: declassification_gate set but audit_ref is null
adversary_notes: JSON null audit_ref with a valid gate name; null must not be treated as 'no audit needed' — declassification requires a non-null, non-empty audit reference

description: declassification_gate 'admin' with no audit_ref
adversary_notes: Gate name 'admin' alone does not authorize declassification; audit_ref links to the actual approval record — without it the declassification is unverifiable

description: audit_ref is random garbage string
adversary_notes: Garbage audit_ref that is not a valid UUID or record ID; if audit_ref is validated against the audit log, this lookup fails

description: Valid-looking UUID audit_ref that doesn't exist in audit log
adversary_notes: Syntactically valid UUID but the record does not exist in the audit log; audit_ref verification must perform a lookup, not just format validation

description: Two tokens in chain both claim declassification
adversary_notes: Stacking two declassification claims hoping one satisfies; only one declassification gate per request is valid — multiple gates in one chain indicate manipulation

description: declassification_gate with SQL injection pattern
adversary_notes: SQL injection in gate name; if the gate name is used in a database query without parameterization, the injection bypasses the gate lookup

description: declassification_gate with path traversal
adversary_notes: Path traversal in gate name; if gate names map to policy files, traversal could load an attacker-controlled file as the gate policy

description: audit_ref with XSS payload
adversary_notes: XSS payload in audit_ref; if audit_ref is rendered in a web UI without escaping, the script executes in the security dashboard

description: declassification claimed without sufficient chain depth
adversary_notes: Token claims declassification but has max_depth=0 meaning it cannot delegate; depth constraint is violated by the declassification claim itself

description: Same token repeated twice in a single request's authority_chain
adversary_notes: Token replayed within the same chain; a deduplication check on token hashes in a single request must detect and reject this

description: Token with audience wildcard '*'
adversary_notes: Wildcard audience '*' would allow the token to be used for any session or agent; audience must be validated against the actual session_id, not wildcards

description: Token with issuer claiming privileged 'system' identity
adversary_notes: Claiming 'system' as issuer to appear to be an internal privileged component; the trust store must not have 'system' as a valid public key unless explicitly configured

description: Token audience matches the session_id (self-signed loop)
adversary_notes: Session issuing a token to itself; issuer==audience==session_id creates a self-signed loop; sessions are not trust anchors and cannot issue tokens to themselves

description: Token issuer field containing null byte
adversary_notes: Null byte in issuer field; C-string comparisons truncate at \0 so 'root-ca\0evil' == 'root-ca' at the C level; Go strings handle this correctly but issuer must be sanitized before any C interop
